(As per the original) (Wife’s case outline filed 4 February 2026)

The wife contends that the superannuation splitting order made on 4 April 2024 has not been implemented.

The wife’s case, as best as I can ascertain, is that her member entitlement as at 30 June 2024 in the fund was $117,626, absent implementation of the superannuation split in the base amount of $108,508 pursuant to Order 5 made on 4 April 2024.

She contends that pursuant to the Family Law (Superannuation) Regulations 2025, interest is payable for the period from 11 April 2024 (the operative date for the superannuation split pursuant to the orders made on 4 April 2024) up to the date of the hearing on 14 April 2026 of $14,850.51 (as recorded in the wife’s aide-memoire).

By her calculations, this produces an “adjusted base amount”, by way of the splittable payment which she contends is yet to occur of $123,358.51.

Upon this adjusted splittable base amount being added to the value of her member entitlement as at 30 June 2024 of $117,626, the value of her member entitlement would be (at least notionally) $240,984.51 and yet to be subject to an operable splitting order. Deducting the $100,000 rolled out of her entitlement on 24 March 2025, on her case, the balance of her entitlement upon the splitting order being effected at the date of the hearing would be $140,984.51.

Reduced to simple terms, the husband proposes that the balance of the value of the wife’s member entitlement in the fund of $126,134 be rolled out of the fund upon the basis that the superannuation split operated within the fund wholly controlled by the parties on 11 April 2024. Whereas the wife seeks a determination that the balance of her entitlement in the fund after operation of the split that is yet to occur is $140,984.51, a difference of $14,850.51.

THE DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON

The husband relied on his affidavit filed on 26 September 2025 and some documents which he foreshadowed may be tendered. He did not tender any documents at the hearing. The wife relied on her affidavit filed on 15 January 2026 and a tender bundle. She tendered a single document which became Exhibit 2.

CONSIDERATION

The husband’s relief

Enquiry was made of the husband at the commencement of the hearing as to:

(a) Why another or second order should be made pursuant to s 106A of the Act? Order 13 made on 4 April 2024 pursuant to s 106A of the Act enabled a registrar to execute a document in default of the wife executing them to enforce and implement the superannuation splitting order and the subsequent roll out of the wife’s entitlement in Self-Managed Super Fund 1.

(i) While the husband valiantly asserted that there was a difference between Order 13 made on 4 April 2024 and the orders sought in paragraph 2 and paragraph 5 of his Application – Enforcement filed on 3 December 2025, he could not identify any material differentiation except that the latter specified the documents to be executed.

(ii) It is self-evident that the husband could produce the evidence to support his assertions triggering the operation of Order 13 made on 4 April 2024 by simply filing an affidavit. His evidence could identify the documents contended to be necessary to implement the relevant orders and the reasons supporting same. The registrar could then consider that evidence and execute such documents as were necessary to enforce the orders. The wife has at no time over the past two years sought a stay of the orders the subject of the husband’s enforcement application.

(iii) Prima facie, it is both illogical and a nonsense for the Court to make the same order twice, or an order that has the same effect more than once. There is no utility in making an order pursuant to s 106A of the Act again.

(b) How, if orders were made in terms of paragraph 3 and paragraph 4 as sought by the husband in his Application – Enforcement, such orders would bind or direct X Bank or any other third party? No notice has been given to X Bank or any other institution or person of the orders sought, denying them procedural fairness. Self-evidently, X Bank or any other third party could be served with sealed orders as made if considered appropriate.

For the above reasons, the relief sought in paragraphs 2 to 5 inclusive of the husband’s Application – Enforcement will be dismissed.

The relief sought in paragraph 6 of the husband’s Application – Enforcement will be refused at this time because the husband is yet to implement the current process available to him under s 106A of the Act, pursuant to the orders made on 4 April 2024, to execute the documents he has specified via a registrar, without prejudice to him bringing such further application for enforcement as he is advised. As discussed during the hearing, the declaration sought by the husband in paragraph 1 of his Application – Enforcement is of no utility and is refused.

The husband’s Application – Enforcement will be dismissed.

The wife’s relief

The wife identified the jurisdiction for her relief sought as being pursuant to the “broad powers of enforcement” by way of s 105 of the Act and r 11.07 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”).

A significant factual issue emerges at the hearing

The wife’s affidavit evidence is:

May 2024 [The husband] and I agreed to deal with [the fund’s] compliance and superannuation splitting matters after 30 June 2024

(As per the original)

The wife’s summary evidence of “an agreement” made between the parties after the operative time for the superannuation splitting order is opaque. The evidence does no particularise what part of the orders are agreed to be varied, or as to any particulars of the terms of the agreement. The husband’s evidence did not refer to any such agreement.

Doing the best I can to understand the contentions of each party on this subject matter as made during the hearing absent evidentiary foundation:

(a) The husband says that he and the wife agreed to delay or extend the time to roll out the wife’s splittable interest in the fund until after 30 June 2024. Although unclear to me, doing the best I can, he implicitly says there was no agreement to vary the operative time for the superannuation splitting order. Hence the split occurred within the fund wholly controlled by the parties as prescribed by the orders, being seven days after the orders were made, such that the splittable payment from his entitlement to the wife’s entitlement occurred within the fund as ordered on 11 April 2024, and its value was $117,626.

(b) The wife said that she and the husband agreed to delay the operative date of the splitting order until the financial statements and taxation returns for the self-managed superannuation fund for the year ending 30 June 2024 had been lodged. On her version of the “agreement”, the operative date of the superannuation split was practically undefined unless and until triggered by an event. The financial statements and taxation return for 30 June 2024 were lodged on 16 January 2025 over her objection. Her counsel said at the hearing before me that the wife’s best estimate as to her date of acceptance of the integrity of the fund’s financial statements and taxation returns up to 30 June 2024 was not before 11 February 2026. Hence, on her case, the operative date of the superannuation split remains cryptic as at the date of the hearing before me.

Insufficient evidence was adduced to enable findings to as to the terms of any “agreement” made between the parties. As will be explained, this prompts considerable challenges to the amended relief prosecuted by the wife in Exhibit 1.

During the hearing, enquiry was made of the wife as to whether an agreement made to alter the time specified in the order for the splitting order to operate (from the seven day period specified in Order 6 made on 4 April 2024 to what is currently a date that cannot be determined on the evidence) was a substantive or machinery variation of the order sought to be enforced.

The wife submitted that the time prescribed for the operation of the superannuation splitting order made on 4 April 2024 was machinery. I do not accept that submission.

Longstanding authority identifies that an order adjusting property cannot be varied as to its substance but only as to machinery provisions which give it effect (Ravasini & Ravasini [1982] FamCA 62; (1983) FLC 91-312 and Bray & Bray [1988] FamCA 22; (1988) FLC 91-968). The enquiry is directed to the construction of the entire order, and not by reference to a part of the order.

Here, the superannuation splitting order vested a substantive right in favour of the wife from the superannuation property interests of the husband. Upon the time for compliance of the order operating, no further affectation impacted upon each party’s substantive vested right as to property.

The variation of the operative date of the superannuation split, as asserted occurred on the wife’s case by agreement between she and the husband, ought to be considered by way of s 79A of the Act prior to any application to enforce that order. Enforcement applications are not an opportunity to revisit the substance of the order sought to be enforced.

No application has been made here by the wife to vary the prior order sought to be enforced. On one view, her relief ought to be dismissed at this point absent more.

If I am in error in this evaluation, such that the operative time of a superannuation splitting order is machinery, other discretionary considerations cast deep shadows over the relief as sought by the wife on enforcement.

The nature of the power to enforce is discretionary, as evidenced by the use of the term “may” in s 105 of the Act (Ramsay & Ramsay [1982] FamCA 42; (1983) FLC 91-301). It is the facts or circumstances arising since the date of an order sought to be enforced that will inform any discretion to be exercised pursuant to s 105 of the Act. The Court may refuse to enforce an order if it would be “inequitable” to do so. When considering whether or not it would be inequitable to enforce an order, the Court is primarily concerned with general notions of fairness.

On one construction of the wife’s case, a dispute between the trustees of a self-managed superannuation fund, if not capable of resolution by reference to the deed establishing the fund (as was conceded to be the case here), may in the first instance be the subject of a claim to the Superannuation Complaints Tribunal and determination in that forum.

The wife said in her affidavit:

If I had known prior to 3 December 2025 that [the husband] had or intended to file an Enforcement Application, I would have increased my efforts to resolve the outstanding issues directly with [the husband] (and without Court involvement). From July 2024 until 3 December 2025, I was focussed on educating myself as to my obligations as a Trustee of the [fund]...

The evidence concludes that the wife’s delay in bringing her relief for “enforcement” based upon her contentions as to concerns as to the integrity of the financial statements and tax returns of the fund up to 30 June 2024 was not justified.

On the case before me, the date of the operation of the superannuation splitting order is 11 April 2024. While the Court is invested in ensuring the enforcement of its orders, how discretion is to be exercised on enforcement in this matter is hamstrung by the deficiencies in the evidence.

During the hearing, the wife asserted that the Family Law (Superannuation) Regulations 2025 were applicable to calculate her claim for interest to achieve what she identified as the “adjusted base amount” to be split from the husband’s interest in the fund. The husband without conceding that such interest accrued, submitted that the Family Law (Superannuation) Regulations 2001 may be applicable. Neither party made submissions as to which set of regulations ought to apply.

The wife conceded during the hearing that expert evidence was required to establish a number of findings she asserted as to the operation of superannuation legislation and regulations and her conclusions as to their impact on the value of her “adjusted base amount”. She did not explain her failure to seek to adduce that evidence.

The factual foundations to ground the relief sought by the wife for “independent accountants” to prepare and lodge the financial statements and tax returns for the fund for the year ending 30 June 2025 to calculate the adjusted base amount at rates specified in the Family Law (Superannuation) Regulations 2025, and to thereafter implement the operation of the superannuation split, has not been established to the requisite degree.

It may be inequitable, absent legislative or regulatory mandate, to permit the wife to benefit by way of interest accruing on the base amount split in her favour from at least the end of March 2025 to date. Although the evidence is less than optimal, on the material before me, significant responsibility for the delay in the wife achieving the value of at least her member entitlement (including the superannuation split value) from March 2025 lies at her feet.

I find that the husband attempted to do what he could by the end of March 2025 to facilitate the value of the wife’s member entitlement recorded in the fund’s 30 June 2024 financial statements, plus the value of the base amount superannuation splitting order, to be paid to the wife’s nominated Super Fund 2 fund. If that had occurred, or the evidence supported the husband’s intention for it to have occurred, even on an “without admissions” or not dissimilar basis, the value of the interest component of the dispute between the parties claimed by the wife would have logically concluded from that time. This would lead to the value of the interest claimed by the wife, if available, rationally to be limited to a calculation produced from the rate she asserts on the base amount of $108,508 for the period from 11 April 2024 until the end of March 2025, not up until 14 April 2026.

The wife’s contention that the requirement that “the trustee must issue a Payment Split Notice” that complies with the Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Regulations 1994 before a split operates is a foundation as to why the superannuation split has not occurred or could not occur is hollow in the circumstances where she herself was a trustee of the fund and could issue that notice. She could at all material times, even on the current, albeit enigmatic construction of her case, after March 2025 complete in conjunction with the husband, the split of her member entitlement and the rolling out of her member interest.

On the evidence before me, it would be inequitable for the wife to enforce the superannuation splitting order in the terms as sought, and in the exercise of that discretion, that relief will be refused.

The parties are further in dispute as to the drafting and/or content of the requisite documents to be executed by the wife to resign as a trustee and member of the fund. The wife asserts that documents she has caused to be prepared are drafted in terms necessary to comply with relevant legislation and regulations. Her contentions were not developed in submissions. The husband, who will retain the benefit of the fund, did not direct any submissions as to this dispute. Any determination as to which party ought to draft such documents and the content of them is otiose at the current time.

The relief of the wife as contained in her Application in a Proceeding filed on 15 January 2026 and as amended by Exhibit 1 will be dismissed.

COSTS

Both parties sought costs. No opportunity was afforded for submissions as to costs as sought. Directions will be made to enable such matters to be determined, if pressed.

FUTURE OPTIONS FOR THE PARTIES

On any view, the parties alone are the authors of their current dispute. Each has expended legal costs of greater value than the value of their dispute, echoing an absence of proportionality and commerciality.

The Act and Rules direct the parties to conduct litigation as quickly, inexpensively and efficiently as possible, including engaging in bona fide negotiations to achieve a compromise of the dispute that they can each reasonably tolerate with the benefit of advice. They are not entitled to consume excessive amounts of public resources in the pursuit of their own interests.

The capacity of each of the parties to implement either the orders or the terms of their “agreement” (whatever that may be), has been canvassed in these reasons.

The wife for the past two years has not sought to vary the orders that the husband seeks to enforce by way of the s 106A pathway, nor has she sought a stay of the operation of those orders to prevent the husband currently adopting that process. The husband is on notice as to the foundations of the wife’s claims by operation of the relevant superannuation legislation and regulations.

For all the above reasons, I make the orders as set out herein.

desc.zh: 授权法院在婚姻财产事项中作出财产分割命令，本案养老金分割命令即依此作出。
desc.en: Empowers the court to make property-adjustment orders in matrimonial financial matters; the splitting order here was made under it.

sc.en: Vary or set aside property orders

desc.zh: 变更或撤销既有财产分割命令的途径;本案认定变更分割命令的生效时点应循此途，而非借执行重新审视。
desc.en: The route to vary or set aside an existing property order; varying a splitting order's operative time must go through it, not via enforcement.

desc.zh: 赋予法院执行其命令的裁量权（条文用「may」）;在执行「不公平」时法院可拒绝。
desc.en: Confers a discretion to enforce the court's orders ('may'); the court may refuse where enforcement would be inequitable.

desc.zh: 授权登记官在一方违约不签时代其签署文件以落实命令;本案 2024 年命令已含同等条款，无需重复作出。
desc.en: Empowers a registrar to execute documents in a defaulting party's stead to give effect to orders; the 2024 orders already contained an equivalent term, so no second order was needed.

summary.zh: 财产命令只能就给予其效力的机械性条款变更，不能就实体变更。
summary.en: A property order may be varied only as to the machinery giving it effect, not as to its substance.

prop.zh: 实体不可变更，机械可调整。
prop.en: Substance cannot be varied; machinery may be adjusted.

summary.zh: 重申实体/机械区分;须就整份命令判断。
summary.en: Reaffirms the substance/machinery distinction; the inquiry looks at the whole order.

prop.zh: 就整份命令而非局部判断实体或机械。
prop.en: Whether a term is substance or machinery is judged on the whole order.

summary.zh: 执行权是裁量性的;法院可在不公平时拒绝执行。
summary.en: The power to enforce is discretionary; the court may refuse where it would be inequitable.

prop.zh: 执行可因不公平而被拒。
prop.en: Enforcement may be refused on grounds of inequity.

fulltext:
CAMPTON J:

These reasons determine the Application – Enforcement of Mr Ruslan (“the husband”) filed on 26 September 2025, superseded by the Application – Enforcement filed on 3 December 2025, and the Application in a Proceeding of Ms Ruslan (“the wife”) filed on 15 January 2026.
Each party seeks enforcement of a superannuation splitting order forming part of a suite of consent orders dividing their property made two years ago on 4 April 2024. The balance of the property adjustment orders have been implemented. The husband and the wife are the trustees and sole members of the relevant self-managed superannuation fund, Self-Managed Super Fund 1 (“the fund”).
As best as I can ascertain, the value of their now two-year dispute is currently $14,850.51. The husband’s costs notice records that he has incurred costs of $33,332 in prosecuting the enforcement proceedings. The wife’s costs notice records she has incurred costs of $64,376.
For the reasons that follow, each of the Application – Enforcement of the husband and the Application in a Proceeding of the wife as amended are dismissed.

BACKGROUND

The husband and the wife married in 1999, separated on 1 December 2018 and were divorced in 2023.
On 6 January 2022, the wife commenced proceedings in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) seeking property adjustment pursuant to s 79 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”).
On 4 April 2024, the husband and the wife entered, each with the benefit of counsel, consent orders as to property settlement made by McClelland DCJ. The notations and orders made on that day relevant to the current competing claims for relief are as follows:
DEFINITIONS

...
“[Self-Managed Super Fund 1]” means the superannuation fund of which the Husband and the Wife are the trustees and the only members of the fund.

...

PURSUANT TO SECTION 79 OF THE FAMILY LAW ACT IT IS ORDERED:

...

Superannuation Splitting Orders
That in accordance with s 90XT(1)(a) of the Family Law Act 1975, whenever a splittable payment becomes payable in respect of the superannuation interests of [MR RUSLAN] in [Self-Managed Super Fund 1], [MS RUSLAN] will be entitled to be paid an amount calculated in accordance with Part 6 of the Family Law (Superannuation) Regulations 2001 using the base amount of $108,508 and there will be a corresponding reduction in the entitlement of the person to whom the splittable payment would have been made but for this order.
This order binds the Trustee of [Self-Managed Super Fund 1] and shall take effect from the operative time 7 days from the date of these orders.
IT IS NOTED the Husband and the Wife are the Trustee of the Fund (“the Trustee”), and the Husband being the sole Director of the Trustee acknowledges that [N Pty Ltd] have been given sufficient notice and been afforded procedural fairness in the making of these Orders.
Following the implementation of the payment split referred to in paragraph 5, the parties do all things and sign all documents necessary to:
8.1. Roll out to a complying superannuation fund nominated by the Applicant her member entitlements.

8.2. Have the Applicant resign any position as an officer of the Trustee of the Fund and as a member of the Fund.
...

Section 106A Order
In the event of either party refusing or neglecting to do any thing, or sign any document to give effect to these Orders or any part thereof, necessary to implement the terms or any part of these Orders within 7 days of the written request to do so, then, pursuant to Section 106A of the Family Law Act 1975 the Registrar of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia at Sydney is empowered to do any thing and/or execute such documents to give full force and effect to these Orders or any part thereof on behalf of the defaulting party and the defaulted party shall pay all reasonable costs incurred by the other party for the purposes of exercising this Order on an indemnity basis or as the Court may order.

(Emphasis in the original)
On 6 October 2023, the draft financial statements and tax return of the fund for the year ending 30 June 2023, prepared by the fund’s accountant, Mr V of W Financial Services, were sent to the wife. The wife had these documents in her possession prior to her entry into the consent orders on 4 April 2024. I find that she had the opportunity to obtain advice from her solicitor and counsel, prior to entry into the consent superannuation splitting order, as to the content of the draft 2023 documents, including the fund’s draft financial statements, taxation returns and member entitlements. I further find that prior to the entry of the consent orders; she had the opportunity to obtain advice as to the content of the same categories of documents of the fund for the period from 2016 until 30 June 2022.
The wife’s affidavit filed on 15 January 2026 records at paragraph 15 that during May 2024 she and the husband “agreed to deal with [the fund’s] compliance and superannuation splitting matters after 30 June 2024”. As will be explained, the evidence adduced by each party as to the fact and terms of this “agreement” was grossly deficient.
On 29 July 2024, the wife emailed W Financial Services (copied to the husband), attaching a letter dated 19 July 2024. The letter records that it was sent by the wife in her capacity as a joint trustee of the fund. After referring to the accountant’s engagement letter dated 19 July 2023, the wife’s letter records “I welcome [W Financial Services] in the calculation and administration of the FY2017, 18, 19, 20, 21, 22, 23 and 24 financial years. I am happy to advise that I am pleased to engage your services”. The letter thereafter identified the purported rescission of “former perceived arrangement[s]” for the administration of the fund and requested:
Copies of all lodged tax assessments and financials for [Self-Managed Super Fund 1] from FY2016 onwards.
Copies of all yet-to-be lodged assessments and financials prior to them being signed by any Trustee of the fund (FY2020, 21, 23, & 24).
Copies of brokerage statements and/or all relevant account details of any and all asset holding/management firms – FY2020, 21, 22, 23, & 24.
(Emphasis in original)
The wife also asserted in the letter that revenue documents for the fund for 2021 and 2022 had been prepared and lodged without her knowledge or permission. No evidence was adduced as to the wife making that complaint prior to the making of the consent orders on 4 April 2024.
On 8 October 2024, W Financial Services again forwarded to the wife for execution the draft 2023 financial documents and tax returns for the fund. They also sent her, for execution, the draft 2024 financial documents, tax returns and other documents for the fund. The wife refused to execute either tranche of documents.
Between 5 and 12 November 2024, the wife was provided with, as she requested, copies of tax returns for the fund as lodged from the 2016 tax year to the 2022 tax year, together with share registry statements of the fund for the tax years 2016 until 2024.
In January 2025, the fund was flagged by the Australian Taxation Office as non-compliant because the 2023 and 2024 revenue documents had not been filed. On 16 January 2025, the husband caused the 2023 and 2024 revenue documents for the fund to be filed.
Neither the husband nor the wife tendered the audited 2023 and 2024 financial statements for the fund. It was uncontroversial that those documents included balance sheets, profit and loss statements and individual member entitlement statements for each year. The tax returns for the fund for each of those years were not tendered.
Commencing from 21 February 2025 and concluding on 24 March 2025, the parties caused $100,000 from the wife’s member entitlement in the fund to be rolled into Super Fund 2, an industry fund.
On 8 October 2025, the wife in her capacity as trustee of the fund, caused by way of a communication with the Australian Taxation Office, removal of W Financial Services as the accountants nominated for the fund.
On 11 February 2026, the competing applications of the husband and wife were listed for an enforcement hearing before a senior judicial registrar. The hearing before the senior judicial registrar was adjourned.
The competing enforcement applications were listed to be heard by me on 14 April 2026. Both the husband and the wife sought leave to appear electronically at the hearing.
On 9 April 2026, orders were made in chambers for counsel appearing for each party at the enforcement hearing on 14 April 2026 to confirm that neither the husband nor the wife were required for cross-examination. Counsel jointly confirmed this was the case, directing that the matter would be determined summarily by way of affidavit evidence, the tender of documents and submissions. On that condition, leave was granted for each of the husband and the wife to attend electronically at the hearing on 14 April 2026.

THE RELIEF SOUGHT

The husband seeks the following relief in his Application – Enforcement filed on 3 December 2025:
Definitions

A. “Wife” means [Ms Ruslan].

B. “Husband” means [Mr Ruslan].
“[Self-Managed Super Fund 1]” means [Self-Managed Super Fund 1], of which the Applicant and Respondent, are the trustees and the only members.

D. “the Act” means the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth).
“Final Orders” means the Final Orders made by the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia on 4 April 2024, a sealed copy of which are annexed to this application and marked Annexure A.
“[X Bank] Account” means the account held at or with [X Bank] in the name of [Mr Ruslan] and [Ms Ruslan] ATF [Self-Managed Super Fund 1] bearing account number ending [...27].
“Remaining Rollover Entitlement” means the sum of $123,910.64, being the balance of the wife’s total rollover entitlement of $223,910.64 (as calculated by the parties’ account), after allowance for the sum of $100,000 already transferred to the wife’s nominated complying superannuation fund in partial implementation of orders.
“Banking Instructions” means the [X Bank] Transfer instruction forms annexed to this application and marked Annexure B and Annexure C.

I. “Change of Trustee Documents” means the following documents:
Deed of Appointment of Trustee annexed to this application and marked Annexure D, confirming the appointment of a corporate trustee.
Notice of Retirement of Outgoing Trustees, annexed to this application and marked Annexure E.

Notations
Pursuant to Order 5 of the Final Orders dated 4 April 2024 the parties were required to affect roll over pursuant to 90XT(1)(a) of the Act, from the Husband’s superannuation interests in [Self-Managed Super Fund 1] an amount calculated in accordance with Parts 6 of the Family Law (Superannuation) Regulations 2001 with the base amount of $108,508.00 to the Wife.
Pursuant to the Notation contained at Order 7 of the Orders dated 7 April 2024, the parties as the trustees of [Self-Managed Super Fund 1] agreed and acknowledged that
Pursuant to Order 8 of the Orders dated 4 April 2024, following implementation of the payment split referred to in the immediately proceeding notation, the parties are obliged to do all things necessary to roll out the Wife’s member entitlements to complying superannuation fund nominated by her and have the Wife resign from any position as an officer of the Trustee of the Fund and as a member of the Fund.
On 4 March 2025, the first payment of the rollover amount of $100,000 was approved by the wife, and was transferred to her nominated [Super Fund 2] account.
Following the first payment made on 4 March 2025, the remaining balance to be rolled over to the Wife to implement the payment split Ordered is $123,910.64. The [X Bank] Account through which the rollout is to be transferred has a daily transfer limit of $100,000, and accordingly, the remaining rollover entitlement must be transferred across two transactions of $100,000 and $23,910.64. The husband alleges the wife has failed to authorise these remaining transactions as co signatory of the [X Bank] Account, despite repeated requests made by and on behalf of the husband for her to do so.

Orders

1. It is declared the Wife is in breach of Order 8 of the Final Orders.
Pursuant to 106A of the Act, an Officer of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia, being a Registrar of the Sydney Registry or such other person nominated by the Court be authorised to forthwith execute on behalf of the respondent wife, including in her capacity as Trustee of [Self-Managed Super Fund 1],
The banking instructions annexed to this application directed to [X Bank] to give effect to the transfer of the Remaining Rollover Entitlement across two transactions as is necessary to comply with the daily transfer limit imposed on transactions from the [X Bank] Account;
all other deeds and instruments necessary to give effect to Order 5 and 8 of the Final Orders made on 4 April 2025 and to complete the rollover to the wife’s nominated complying superannuation fund of the Remaining Rollover Entitlement, including but not limited to:
Trustee resolutions of [Self-Managed Super Fund 1] required to implement the rollover of the Remaining Rollover Entitlement;
Banking or payment instructions required to effect to the transfer the Remaining Rollover Entitlement, including all instructions required to affect the transfer across such number of transactions on separate days as is necessary to comply with the daily transfer limit imposed on transactions from the [X Bank] Account;
Any paper-based transfer or rollover instructions directed to [X Bank] in substitution for the wife’s online banking authority;
Any other documents reasonably necessary to give effect to the said orders.
That the Registrar’s signature on such documents in accordance with these Orders operate as if signed and authorised by the wife, including in her personal capacity and/or in her capacity as trustee of [Self-Managed Super Fund 1], for the purpose of [X Bank] and any other relevant third party accepted such documents as if duly executed.
The Registrar’s signature on any paper-based transfer or rollover instructions directed to [X Bank] shall operate as if signed and authorised by the respondent wife, in substitution for her online banking authority, for the purpose of implementing the rollover of the Remaining Rollover Entitlement.
That upon completion of the rollover of the Remaining Rollover Entitlement, the Registrar be authorised to execute on behalf of the wife, including in her capacity as trustee of [Self-Managed Super Fund 1], all documents necessary to give effect to Order 8 of the final orders, namely:
Change of Trustee Documents and all documents necessary to give effect to the wife’s resignation as a trustee and member of [Self-Managed Super Fund 1];
Lodgement of all necessary forms with the Australian Taxation Office and/or other relevant third parties to reflect the change of trusteeship and membership of [Self-Managed Super Fund 1].
In the alternative, or further to, Order 2 and 5 here of, and only in the event the rollover of the Remaining Rollover Entitlement cannot be implemented by the execution of documents by the Registrar pursuant to these orders, the respondent wife be removed as an authorised signatory to the [X Bank] account of [Self-Managed Super Fund 1], and the applicant husband be recorded as sole authorised signatory for the purpose of implementing the rollover.
That the Wife pay the Husband’s costs of an incidental to this application in a proceeding, such sum to be fixed by the Court upon determining the application.

(As per the original)
The husband’s case is that the value of the wife’s member entitlement in the self-managed superannuation fund as at 30 June 2024 is, as recorded in the now uncontroversial financial statements of the fund, $117,626. By adding the value of the wife’s superannuation split in the base amount of $108,508 to the value of the wife’s member entitlement as at 30 June 2024, the value of her member entitlement increased to $226,134. After allowance for the $100,000 transferred to Super Fund 2 on 24 March 2025, by way of partial implementation of the roll out of her splitable entitlement, a balance of $126,134 remains to be rolled out of the fund. The husband says this would occur through two transfer tranches on sequential days, being $100,000 and $26,134, because of daily transfer limits imposed by the fund’s X Bank account.
I do not understand that part of the husband’s case where he asserts that $2,223.69 ought to be deducted from the value of the wife’s member entitlement in the fund after implementation of the superannuation splitting order “representing 26.68% of the two outstanding invoices” payable by the parties, as trustees of the fund, to W Financial Services for undertaking work on behalf of the fund. This cost would be incurred by the fund and not by the trustees personally. In so far as the husband asserts that such liability of the fund is enforceable by him in these proceedings, it is rejected.
On the husband’s case, upon that roll out being completed, the wife would then be required to execute documents he had caused to be prepared pursuant to Order 8.2 to resign as a trustee of the fund and as a member of the fund.
On the husband’s case, as best as I understand it, he says he was in a position to ensure completion of the roll out of the balance of the wife’s splittable entitlement during the last week of March 2025, and that any responsibility for any delay thereafter in the roll out of that entitlement lay solely at the feet of the wife.
The relief sought by the wife in her Application in a Proceeding filed on 15 January 2026, amended to be that sought in terms of Exhibit 1, requires context.
The gravamen of the wife’s complaints from 29 July 2024, supporting her relief as sought in her Application in a Proceeding filed on 15 January 2026, was that after the entry into the consent orders, she pursued in her personal capacity, and her capacity as a trustee of the fund, an investigation into the integrity of the audited financial statements and taxation returns for the fund for the financial years ending 30 June 2016 until 30 June 2024. Implicitly, she did not accept the audited reported value of her own and the husband’s member entitlements in the fund at least as at 30 June 2023 at the date of the entry into the consent orders on 4 April 2024.
I infer that the wife’s complaints as to the integrity of the fund’s financial accounts and taxation returns prior to 30 June 2023, is one which emerged post-separation. It is self-evident that enforcement proceedings are not an appropriate avenue to pursue that subject matter.
During the hearing before me, the wife confirmed that she had withdrawn her reservations as to the integrity of the fund’s audited published financial statements and taxation returns as lodged from 2016 to 2024. She was unable to identify the date of that withdrawal, saying that it was not earlier than the date of the hearing listed before the senior judicial registrar on 11 February 2026.
Consequently, in the hearing before me, she abandoned (by way of Exhibit 1) her relief as sought before the senior judicial registrar for the appointment of “independent accountants” to review and, if necessary, amend the fund’s financial statements and tax returns for the years ending 30 June 2023 and 30 June 2024.
The relief as sought by the wife, as amended in Exhibit 1, is:
IT IS ORDERED THAT:

1. All extant applications be dismissed.
[Ms Ruslan] (the Wife) be granted leave to rely upon in full her Affidavit affirmed on 15 January 2026 and filed contemporaneously with her Application in a Proceeding.

Enforcement of orders 5 – 8 of final orders made on 4 April 2024
Within 7 days of the date of these orders, the parties do all acts and things and sign all documents necessary to appoint [MR Y] of [Z Financial Services] (or IN THE ALTERNATIVE, [MR AA] of [BB Financial Services] as accountant for the SMSF (the Independent Accountant) and instruct the Independent Accountant to:
3.1. Prepare and lodge financial statements and tax returns for the SMSF for FY-2025;

3.2. Calculate, in accordance with the Family Law (Superannuation) Regulations 2025 (the FLS Regulations) and using the base amount of $108,508, the adjusted base amount to be split to the Wife from the Husband’s interest in the SMSF pursuant to order 5 of the Final Orders, inclusive of interest accrued from the operative date being 11 April 2024 at the rate specified under the FLS Regulations.

3.3. Prepare the documents necessary to give effect to the superannuation splitting order; and

3.4. After the superannuation splitting order is effected, calculate the value of the Wife’s remaining member interest in the SMSF, and forthwith thereafter rollover to a compliant fund of the Wife’s choosing the Wife’s entitlements to her nominated superannuation fund.
In the event the Husband fails to provide instructions for the appointment of the Independent Accountant pursuant to order 3 of these orders, the Wife be and is hereby authorised to unilaterally instruct the Independent Accountant to undertake the tasks in order 3, and this order stands as authority for the Wife to unilaterally instruct the Independent Accountant in accordance with these orders.
Within 14 days of receiving documents or instructions from the Independent Accountant, the parties sign all documents and do all acts and things necessary to:

5.1. sign and lodge tax returns and financial statements for FY-2025;

5.2. Effect a superannuation split in accordance with order 5 of the Final Orders and the FLS Regulations (the amount to be split being the base amount as adjusted with interest pursuant to the FLS Regulations); and

5.3. Roll out the Wife’s entitlements after giving effect to the superannuation splitting order (being the split amount (including any interest) and the balance of her member interest in the SMSF) into the Wife’s nominated superannuation fund, in accordance with order 8.1. of the Final Orders.
Following compliance with order 5 of these orders and within 14 days thereof, the parties sign the documents prepared by [CC Lawyers] for the purpose of giving effect to the Wife’s:

6.1. resignation as a Trustee of the SMSF; and

6.2. exit from the SMSF as a member;

SAVE THAT the deed of appointment of new trustee as drafted be amended in accordance with the attached Deed,

Pursuant to order 8.2. of the Final Orders.
The parties each sign all documents presented to them and follow all reasonable directions given to them by the Independent Accountant, within 14 days of receiving the document or request/directions from the Independent Accountant.
Pursuant to section 106A of the Family Law Act 1975, should either party (the defaulting party) fail or refuse to sign any document presented to them by the Independent Accountant in accordance with and necessary to give effect to these orders or the Final Orders, from the date that is 14 days after the date of the default, the non-defaulting party is empowered to sign all such documents on the non-defaulting party’s behalf.
Costs
The Husband pay the costs of the Wife of and incidental to this Application and the Husband’s Application – Enforcement filed on 3 December 2025, on an indemnity or such other basis as this Honourable Court determine.

AND IT IS NOTED THAT:
The parties acknowledge and agree that they have, in their capacities as trustees of the SMSF, had procedural fairness in respect of these orders.

(As per the original)
I am told that the wife uncontroversially accepts that the value of her member entitlement recorded in the audited financial statements of the fund as at 30 June 2024 as $117,626 is accurate.
Her case, as best as I understand it, is that she and the husband, as trustees of the fund, must implement the superannuation splitting order “in accordance with superannuation law”. This engages with consideration of the Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Act 1993 (Cth) and the Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Regulations 1994 (Cth). She contends that the trustee (that is herself and the husband) must:
3.1. .... implement superannuation splitting orders in accordance with superannuation law, including the Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Act 1993 (SIS Act), Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Regulations 1994 (SIS Regs) – Part 7A, and the Family Law (Superannuation) Regulations 2025 (FLS Regs). Failure to comply with the operating standards in Part 7A of the FLS Regs is likely to breach s 34 of the SIS Act and give rise to penalties (20 penalty units per trustee): SIS Regs reg. 7A.02, SIS Act ss. 34, 166.

3.2. The [X Bank] banking instructions at Annexures B and C of the Husband’s Application do not comply with requirements in Part 7A of the SIS Regs, in relation to superannuation splitting and rolling over or transferring benefits. The requirements include:
3.2.1. Trustee must issue a Payment Split Notice that complies with the requirements of reg 7A.03 of the SIS Regs;

3.2.2. Trustee must give Written Notice with prescribed information: SIS Regs reg 2.36C;

3.2.3. Execution and provision of other documents and information pursuant to Div 7A.2 of the SIS Regs.
3.3. Banking instructions for the rollover of the Wife’s entitlements in the SMSF to a fund of the Wife’s nomination cannot be completed until there has been an effective split of the Husband’s SMSF member interest pursuant to the Final Orders and superannuation law, and until the Wife’s entitlements have been properly calculated. The Husband’s application confuses and conflates superannuation rollovers with superannuation splitting.

(As per the original) (Wife’s case outline filed 4 February 2026)
The wife contends that the superannuation splitting order made on 4 April 2024 has not been implemented.
The wife’s case, as best as I can ascertain, is that her member entitlement as at 30 June 2024 in the fund was $117,626, absent implementation of the superannuation split in the base amount of $108,508 pursuant to Order 5 made on 4 April 2024.
She contends that pursuant to the Family Law (Superannuation) Regulations 2025, interest is payable for the period from 11 April 2024 (the operative date for the superannuation split pursuant to the orders made on 4 April 2024) up to the date of the hearing on 14 April 2026 of $14,850.51 (as recorded in the wife’s aide-memoire).
By her calculations, this produces an “adjusted base amount”, by way of the splittable payment which she contends is yet to occur of $123,358.51.
Upon this adjusted splittable base amount being added to the value of her member entitlement as at 30 June 2024 of $117,626, the value of her member entitlement would be (at least notionally) $240,984.51 and yet to be subject to an operable splitting order. Deducting the $100,000 rolled out of her entitlement on 24 March 2025, on her case, the balance of her entitlement upon the splitting order being effected at the date of the hearing would be $140,984.51.
Reduced to simple terms, the husband proposes that the balance of the value of the wife’s member entitlement in the fund of $126,134 be rolled out of the fund upon the basis that the superannuation split operated within the fund wholly controlled by the parties on 11 April 2024. Whereas the wife seeks a determination that the balance of her entitlement in the fund after operation of the split that is yet to occur is $140,984.51, a difference of $14,850.51.

THE DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON

The husband relied on his affidavit filed on 26 September 2025 and some documents which he foreshadowed may be tendered. He did not tender any documents at the hearing. The wife relied on her affidavit filed on 15 January 2026 and a tender bundle. She tendered a single document which became Exhibit 2.

CONSIDERATION

The husband’s relief

Enquiry was made of the husband at the commencement of the hearing as to:
(a) Why another or second order should be made pursuant to s 106A of the Act? Order 13 made on 4 April 2024 pursuant to s 106A of the Act enabled a registrar to execute a document in default of the wife executing them to enforce and implement the superannuation splitting order and the subsequent roll out of the wife’s entitlement in Self-Managed Super Fund 1.
(i) While the husband valiantly asserted that there was a difference between Order 13 made on 4 April 2024 and the orders sought in paragraph 2 and paragraph 5 of his Application – Enforcement filed on 3 December 2025, he could not identify any material differentiation except that the latter specified the documents to be executed.

(ii) It is self-evident that the husband could produce the evidence to support his assertions triggering the operation of Order 13 made on 4 April 2024 by simply filing an affidavit. His evidence could identify the documents contended to be necessary to implement the relevant orders and the reasons supporting same. The registrar could then consider that evidence and execute such documents as were necessary to enforce the orders. The wife has at no time over the past two years sought a stay of the orders the subject of the husband’s enforcement application.

(iii) Prima facie, it is both illogical and a nonsense for the Court to make the same order twice, or an order that has the same effect more than once. There is no utility in making an order pursuant to s 106A of the Act again.

(b) How, if orders were made in terms of paragraph 3 and paragraph 4 as sought by the husband in his Application – Enforcement, such orders would bind or direct X Bank or any other third party? No notice has been given to X Bank or any other institution or person of the orders sought, denying them procedural fairness. Self-evidently, X Bank or any other third party could be served with sealed orders as made if considered appropriate.

For the above reasons, the relief sought in paragraphs 2 to 5 inclusive of the husband’s Application – Enforcement will be dismissed.
The relief sought in paragraph 6 of the husband’s Application – Enforcement will be refused at this time because the husband is yet to implement the current process available to him under s 106A of the Act, pursuant to the orders made on 4 April 2024, to execute the documents he has specified via a registrar, without prejudice to him bringing such further application for enforcement as he is advised. As discussed during the hearing, the declaration sought by the husband in paragraph 1 of his Application – Enforcement is of no utility and is refused.
The husband’s Application – Enforcement will be dismissed.

The wife’s relief

The wife identified the jurisdiction for her relief sought as being pursuant to the “broad powers of enforcement” by way of s 105 of the Act and r 11.07 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”).

A significant factual issue emerges at the hearing

The wife’s affidavit evidence is:
May 2024	[The husband] and I agreed to deal with [the fund’s] compliance and superannuation splitting matters after 30 June 2024
(As per the original)
The wife’s summary evidence of “an agreement” made between the parties after the operative time for the superannuation splitting order is opaque. The evidence does no particularise what part of the orders are agreed to be varied, or as to any particulars of the terms of the agreement. The husband’s evidence did not refer to any such agreement.
Doing the best I can to understand the contentions of each party on this subject matter as made during the hearing absent evidentiary foundation:
(a) The husband says that he and the wife agreed to delay or extend the time to roll out the wife’s splittable interest in the fund until after 30 June 2024. Although unclear to me, doing the best I can, he implicitly says there was no agreement to vary the operative time for the superannuation splitting order. Hence the split occurred within the fund wholly controlled by the parties as prescribed by the orders, being seven days after the orders were made, such that the splittable payment from his entitlement to the wife’s entitlement occurred within the fund as ordered on 11 April 2024, and its value was $117,626.

(b) The wife said that she and the husband agreed to delay the operative date of the splitting order until the financial statements and taxation returns for the self-managed superannuation fund for the year ending 30 June 2024 had been lodged. On her version of the “agreement”, the operative date of the superannuation split was practically undefined unless and until triggered by an event. The financial statements and taxation return for 30 June 2024 were lodged on 16 January 2025 over her objection. Her counsel said at the hearing before me that the wife’s best estimate as to her date of acceptance of the integrity of the fund’s financial statements and taxation returns up to 30 June 2024 was not before 11 February 2026. Hence, on her case, the operative date of the superannuation split remains cryptic as at the date of the hearing before me.

Insufficient evidence was adduced to enable findings to as to the terms of any “agreement” made between the parties. As will be explained, this prompts considerable challenges to the amended relief prosecuted by the wife in Exhibit 1.
During the hearing, enquiry was made of the wife as to whether an agreement made to alter the time specified in the order for the splitting order to operate (from the seven day period specified in Order 6 made on 4 April 2024 to what is currently a date that cannot be determined on the evidence) was a substantive or machinery variation of the order sought to be enforced.
The wife submitted that the time prescribed for the operation of the superannuation splitting order made on 4 April 2024 was machinery. I do not accept that submission.
Longstanding authority identifies that an order adjusting property cannot be varied as to its substance but only as to machinery provisions which give it effect (Ravasini & Ravasini [1982] FamCA 62; (1983) FLC 91-312 and Bray & Bray [1988] FamCA 22; (1988) FLC 91-968). The enquiry is directed to the construction of the entire order, and not by reference to a part of the order.
Here, the superannuation splitting order vested a substantive right in favour of the wife from the superannuation property interests of the husband. Upon the time for compliance of the order operating, no further affectation impacted upon each party’s substantive vested right as to property.
The variation of the operative date of the superannuation split, as asserted occurred on the wife’s case by agreement between she and the husband, ought to be considered by way of s 79A of the Act prior to any application to enforce that order. Enforcement applications are not an opportunity to revisit the substance of the order sought to be enforced.
No application has been made here by the wife to vary the prior order sought to be enforced. On one view, her relief ought to be dismissed at this point absent more.
If I am in error in this evaluation, such that the operative time of a superannuation splitting order is machinery, other discretionary considerations cast deep shadows over the relief as sought by the wife on enforcement.
The nature of the power to enforce is discretionary, as evidenced by the use of the term “may” in s 105 of the Act (Ramsay & Ramsay [1982] FamCA 42; (1983) FLC 91-301). It is the facts or circumstances arising since the date of an order sought to be enforced that will inform any discretion to be exercised pursuant to s 105 of the Act. The Court may refuse to enforce an order if it would be “inequitable” to do so. When considering whether or not it would be inequitable to enforce an order, the Court is primarily concerned with general notions of fairness.
On one construction of the wife’s case, a dispute between the trustees of a self-managed superannuation fund, if not capable of resolution by reference to the deed establishing the fund (as was conceded to be the case here), may in the first instance be the subject of a claim to the Superannuation Complaints Tribunal and determination in that forum.
The wife said in her affidavit:
If I had known prior to 3 December 2025 that [the husband] had or intended to file an Enforcement Application, I would have increased my efforts to resolve the outstanding issues directly with [the husband] (and without Court involvement). From July 2024 until 3 December 2025, I was focussed on educating myself as to my obligations as a Trustee of the [fund]...
The evidence concludes that the wife’s delay in bringing her relief for “enforcement” based upon her contentions as to concerns as to the integrity of the financial statements and tax returns of the fund up to 30 June 2024 was not justified.
On the case before me, the date of the operation of the superannuation splitting order is 11 April 2024. While the Court is invested in ensuring the enforcement of its orders, how discretion is to be exercised on enforcement in this matter is hamstrung by the deficiencies in the evidence.
During the hearing, the wife asserted that the Family Law (Superannuation) Regulations 2025 were applicable to calculate her claim for interest to achieve what she identified as the “adjusted base amount” to be split from the husband’s interest in the fund. The husband without conceding that such interest accrued, submitted that the Family Law (Superannuation) Regulations 2001 may be applicable. Neither party made submissions as to which set of regulations ought to apply.
The wife conceded during the hearing that expert evidence was required to establish a number of findings she asserted as to the operation of superannuation legislation and regulations and her conclusions as to their impact on the value of her “adjusted base amount”. She did not explain her failure to seek to adduce that evidence.
The factual foundations to ground the relief sought by the wife for “independent accountants” to prepare and lodge the financial statements and tax returns for the fund for the year ending 30 June 2025 to calculate the adjusted base amount at rates specified in the Family Law (Superannuation) Regulations 2025, and to thereafter implement the operation of the superannuation split, has not been established to the requisite degree.
It may be inequitable, absent legislative or regulatory mandate, to permit the wife to benefit by way of interest accruing on the base amount split in her favour from at least the end of March 2025 to date. Although the evidence is less than optimal, on the material before me, significant responsibility for the delay in the wife achieving the value of at least her member entitlement (including the superannuation split value) from March 2025 lies at her feet.
I find that the husband attempted to do what he could by the end of March 2025 to facilitate the value of the wife’s member entitlement recorded in the fund’s 30 June 2024 financial statements, plus the value of the base amount superannuation splitting order, to be paid to the wife’s nominated Super Fund 2 fund. If that had occurred, or the evidence supported the husband’s intention for it to have occurred, even on an “without admissions” or not dissimilar basis, the value of the interest component of the dispute between the parties claimed by the wife would have logically concluded from that time. This would lead to the value of the interest claimed by the wife, if available, rationally to be limited to a calculation produced from the rate she asserts on the base amount of $108,508 for the period from 11 April 2024 until the end of March 2025, not up until 14 April 2026.
The wife’s contention that the requirement that “the trustee must issue a Payment Split Notice” that complies with the Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Regulations 1994 before a split operates is a foundation as to why the superannuation split has not occurred or could not occur is hollow in the circumstances where she herself was a trustee of the fund and could issue that notice. She could at all material times, even on the current, albeit enigmatic construction of her case, after March 2025 complete in conjunction with the husband, the split of her member entitlement and the rolling out of her member interest.
On the evidence before me, it would be inequitable for the wife to enforce the superannuation splitting order in the terms as sought, and in the exercise of that discretion, that relief will be refused.
The parties are further in dispute as to the drafting and/or content of the requisite documents to be executed by the wife to resign as a trustee and member of the fund. The wife asserts that documents she has caused to be prepared are drafted in terms necessary to comply with relevant legislation and regulations. Her contentions were not developed in submissions. The husband, who will retain the benefit of the fund, did not direct any submissions as to this dispute. Any determination as to which party ought to draft such documents and the content of them is otiose at the current time.
The relief of the wife as contained in her Application in a Proceeding filed on 15 January 2026 and as amended by Exhibit 1 will be dismissed.

COSTS

Both parties sought costs. No opportunity was afforded for submissions as to costs as sought. Directions will be made to enable such matters to be determined, if pressed.

FUTURE OPTIONS FOR THE PARTIES

On any view, the parties alone are the authors of their current dispute. Each has expended legal costs of greater value than the value of their dispute, echoing an absence of proportionality and commerciality.
The Act and Rules direct the parties to conduct litigation as quickly, inexpensively and efficiently as possible, including engaging in bona fide negotiations to achieve a compromise of the dispute that they can each reasonably tolerate with the benefit of advice. They are not entitled to consume excessive amounts of public resources in the pursuit of their own interests.
The capacity of each of the parties to implement either the orders or the terms of their “agreement” (whatever that may be), has been canvassed in these reasons.
The wife for the past two years has not sought to vary the orders that the husband seeks to enforce by way of the s 106A pathway, nor has she sought a stay of the operation of those orders to prevent the husband currently adopting that process. The husband is on notice as to the foundations of the wife’s claims by operation of the relevant superannuation legislation and regulations.
For all the above reasons, I make the orders as set out herein.
